variants.warrior:
The portal closes behind you with a sound like breaking ice. You were supposed to come through with three others. The clearing holds only you, your spear, and the particular silence of a place that has been watching the road for a very long time.

Your training says: assess the situation. You do. Trees too dense, wrong species for the elevation. Ambient temperature higher than any chart of the Marches prepared you for. No sounds of the others. No sounds, in fact, of anything that should be making sounds in old-growth forest.

The road is ahead of you. There is no other direction.
variants.scholar:
Your instruments register the anomaly before your eyes do. The portal's resonance is wrong — dispersive rather than channeled. The others went somewhere. Possibly here. Possibly not.

You open your notebook and begin recording, because documenting the disaster is still better than simply experiencing it. Observation one: you are alone. Observation two: the tree species here don't match any catalogued distribution pattern for this region. Observation three: the air has a quality that your instruments have no category for.

You write 'unknown compound, sweetish, not unpleasant' and then close the notebook, because you have run out of things to write that aren't about how quiet it is.
variants.scout:
Three seconds to assess. One: you're alone. Two: the tree line is wrong — pines this dense don't grow this far into the Warming Frost on any map you've memorized. Three: something in those trees already knows you're here.

You go still and start counting exits. There is one: the road ahead.

Four: the silence is organized. Wild things make noise. Wild things make noise continuously, as a matter of fact, and the absence of that noise is itself a kind of information. Something has been keeping the forest quiet. Something that is no longer the thing it is keeping the forest quiet for.

Now it is waiting for you.

acts.title: The Waystation
acts.subtitle: Learn to move through this place.
acts.mechanic_hint: The Explore action lets you search your current region for events, items, and threats. Try it now.

variants.warrior:
The road surface is wrong. You notice it before you notice anything else — a warrior's instinct for ground, for footing, for what the terrain will do to your ankles if you need to move fast. The stone has been maintained, but by something methodical rather than human. No frost heave at the edges, no settlement. Whatever keeps this road has kept it for longer than any garrison would.

The waystation resolves from the tree line as a defensible position: single entrance, stone walls, no upper story to worry about. You approach the door from the angle of least exposure and take in the room in one sweep. Field table, cold fire ring, duty roster. Signs of a rapid departure, not a fight. The two people who wrote those margin notes left under their own power. That matters.

The Warden exit marker scratched behind the table is the only thing in this room that feels like good news. It is not especially good news. But it is what you have.
variants.scholar:
The road is a data point. The stone is too well-maintained for the documented patrol schedules of this region — you check your notes to be certain — and the tree species pressing in from either side represent at least four genera that your catalogues place two hundred kilometers apart in separate ecological zones. You write this down. You write everything down, because the alternative is simply experiencing a cascade of inexplicable phenomena with nothing to show for it.

The waystation provides more data. The duty roster is eighteen months old, but the second handwriting in the margins — more cramped, more urgent — was added significantly later. Two people were here after the garrison withdrew, long enough to record observations, not long enough to finish recording them. The interrupted sentence is the most interesting artifact: 'The corruption has a preferred direction.' This is a testable hypothesis. You underline it twice.

The Warden exit marker is not data, exactly. It is a conclusion: they survived long enough to mark their exit. You allow yourself to find this encouraging before returning to your instruments.
variants.scout:
You cover the first two hundred meters in a controlled walk, reading the road as you move. No fresh tracks beyond your own. The tree line has been quiet too long — the absence of bird sound at this hour means something has been here recently and the forest is still deciding it has left. You watch the understory for movement and find none, which is itself a form of movement: things that are not moving on purpose.

The waystation has a sight line problem — the approach from the north is blind past the stone corner — but the door shows no forced entry and the interior registers as clear before you step through. You learn what you need to from the room in seconds: cold fire ring, recent occupation, panicked exit. The duty roster confirms the timeline. Two survivors, judging by the two handwriting styles in the margins. The last line of their notes is a warning with the ending cut off.

The Warden exit marker behind the table is how you know they made it. You find the angle of the scratch, gauge the direction it points, and file it.

steps.result: The road is older than it looks — the surface has been maintained by something that is not weather. The trees on either side press inward at angles that suggest they have been doing this for a long time and intend to continue. Twenty minutes of walking produces no ambush, which you note as meaningful rather than reassuring.

The waystation is a single-room stone structure with a door that was last used recently, by someone in a hurry. Inside: a field table, a cold fire ring, and a duty roster pinned to the wall.

Three names. An eighteen-month-old date. The notes in the margin are in two different hands, the second more cramped than the first, as if the second person was running low on page and options simultaneously.

The duty roster ends mid-sentence. The last entry reads: 'The corruption has a preferred direction. We thought it was diffuse but it — '

That is where the writing stops. The ink is faded but the interruption is not weathering. Something interrupted it.

You find, behind the table, a scratched mark on the stone that you recognize as a Warden Order exit marker. They made it out. You note this with more relief than you expected.

acts.title: The Imp
acts.subtitle: Not everything here attacks.
acts.mechanic_hint: Some encounters offer choices before combat. What you do next shapes how the scene resolves.

variants.warrior:
The thing on the road is not a threat. You have assessed threats for long enough to know the shape of one, and this does not have it. It is small, corrupted by the look of its edges, and it is sitting with the self-conscious stillness of something that heard your footstep and decided the best response was to pretend it had been sitting here all along.

You stop. You do not reach for your weapon. A spear is the wrong tool for this, and besides — if this creature were capable of harm at this scale, it would have fled rather than frozen. It is afraid of you. The fact that it is also curious is the more interesting data point. You watch it and consider what it wants.
variants.scholar:
Remarkable. You have read three accounts of corrupted imps in your preparatory research, and none of them mentioned this: the creature is clearly aware of you, and it is not running.

You stop walking and begin observing. The posture suggests recent stillness adopted deliberately — it heard you and chose to hold position rather than flee, which implies either insufficient threat assessment or something more complex. The corruption manifests in its edges, a characteristic visual diffusion you have seen in your reference plates. But the behavior is anomalous. Most corrupted creatures in your sources either attack or scatter. This one appears to be waiting to see what you do.

You find your notebook before you find a decision. That is the correct order.
variants.scout:
You smell it before you see it — a faint organic note underneath the pine-resin, something that doesn't belong to the forest's regular lexicon. You slow your pace and let the road tell you more. Then you see it: a corrupted imp, road-center, back to you, sitting with the frozen deliberateness of prey that has already been spotted and knows it.

You stop and read the situation through your senses rather than your assumptions. Wind is from the northwest — it caught your scent before your sound. It is still here. That means it has weighed the option of running and found something about remaining preferable. Curiosity, possibly. Or it is habituated to travelers and associates your silhouette with something other than danger.

You stay still and let it decide.

acts.setup: A single corrupted Imp sits on the road ahead, back to you. It is not doing anything threatening. It is sitting with the concentrated stillness of something that has just become aware of something behind it but has not yet decided what to do about that.

choices.label: Chase it
choices.result:
It runs. It runs fast and low and with a strange loping efficiency. You follow it as far as the tree line, where it simply disappears into a gap between roots.

Where it was sitting: a small arrangement of river stones and a shard of polished obsidian. The obsidian is warm. It is also, faintly, luminous.

choices.label: Approach slowly
choices.result:
It allows three steps before it bolts. Two steps. One. Then it is gone, with a sound like a question mark.

Where it was sitting: a small arrangement of river stones and a shard of polished obsidian. The obsidian is warm. It is also, faintly, luminous.

choices.label: Wait and observe
choices.result:
You wait. It waits. You wait longer. It makes a sound that is not quite language and then leaves with more dignity than the situation strictly warrants.

Where it was: a small arrangement of river stones and a shard of polished obsidian. The obsidian is warm. It is also, faintly, luminous.

item_found.name: Warm Obsidian
item_found.desc: A shard of obsidian that radiates warmth and faint light. Corrupted in origin. Possibly useful. Definitely unsettling.

acts.title: The Wolf
acts.subtitle: Learn what losing means here.
acts.mechanic_hint: When you fight, you choose how hard to press. Strike deals damage. Defend reduces incoming hits. Watch your HP.
acts.setup: A lone corrupted wolf blocks the road. It is larger than a normal wolf by a third. Its eyes are the wrong color — amber-gold with too much intelligence behind them. It has been watching you approach.
acts.win_scene:
It goes down on the third strike. Not dead — injured, backing away, that same amber intelligence watching you from a safer distance.

Then it does something a wolf should not do. It hesitates. It turns toward you, ears flat, and holds the position for three full seconds — the posture of something that has been given a command and is choosing, just now, whether to execute it.

It chooses not to. It goes into the forest. You note that something told it to stop. You note that you do not know what that something is.

win_variants.warrior:
You put it down on the third strike — controlled, economical. A clean exchange: you gave it two openings and it took one, and the one it took was the one you could afford. It is backing away now, still watching, and you let it go because finishing it would cost more than it is worth.

But then it stops. It should not stop. An injured animal in retreat does not stop. It turns back to you with ears flat and holds a posture that your body reads before your mind does — the posture of something receiving a command through channels you cannot observe. It holds for three seconds. Then it decides against whatever it was told to do and disappears into the trees.

You stay in a guard stance for a count of ten after it goes. Someone was watching through that wolf's eyes. Someone made a decision about you just now. The thought does not leave you for the rest of the afternoon.
win_variants.scholar:
Three strikes to bring it down — you tracked the pattern as it fought, and the third strike was a hypothesis confirmed. It retreats now, wounded, and you are already forming notes about its behavior: the amber eyes, the intelligence behind them, the way its tactics adapted rather than simply escalating. This is not a feral animal operating on instinct. Something has organized it.

Then it stops retreating. This is the most interesting data point of the entire encounter. It turns, ears flat, and holds a posture you have no entry for in any field guide. Three full seconds. Its gaze does not have the quality of an animal looking at a threat. It has the quality of an instrument receiving a reading.

Then it goes. You write: 'Someone looked through its eyes. Assessed. Withdrew.' You underline 'Someone' twice and stare at it for a long moment before continuing.
win_variants.scout:
You read the fight the way you read terrain — three moves ahead, trading ground for angles. The wolf is fast and its patterns are wrong for normal predation, too considered, too patient. You win by giving it a choice it cannot take and then closing the gap while it chooses. Three strikes. Clean enough.

It falls back, and you let it go — a retreating wolf is not a threat worth chasing. But it stops. Thirty meters out, it stops and turns, and you go very still because what you are looking at is not a wounded animal recalculating. You have watched animals recalculate. This is something receiving information from a direction you cannot triangulate.

Three seconds. Then it vanishes into the understory without sound. You stay in position, listening, reading the forest's response to its passage. The trees go quiet again. Something at a distance has been informed that you are here. You have no way to know what it was told.

acts.loss_scene:
The wolf does not kill you.

This is the relevant fact. It takes you down with enough force to establish the point — you are on the ground, winded, unable to counter — and then it does something that will occupy the back of your mind for the next several days.

It stands over you. It waits. And then, after a period of time that your nervous system will catalog as the most significant of your recent life, it breathes out through its nose and steps back. Deliberate. Patient. Like it was checking something off a list.

You lie in the road and try to understand what just happened. The corruption enters the bruises first — you can feel it, a warmth that has nothing to do with healing. The wolf is gone by the time you can sit up.

loss_variants.warrior:
You lost the exchange. This is the fact you have to sit with while you are on the ground. You made no critical errors — the wolf was simply operating at a level that your training did not prepare you for, coordinated and patient in a way that predators are not. It took you down on the third pass and then it did the thing you cannot stop thinking about: it stopped.

It stands over you. You try to move and find that your body has submitted before your mind has, a martial assessment overriding your intentions — you are beaten, and the body knows it. The wolf waits. Its amber eyes study you with the attention of something that is not deciding whether to finish this but whether you are what it was looking for.

Then it breathes out and steps away. Deliberate. Patient. You have been weighed.

The corruption comes in through the bruises while you are still lying there, a warmth that your body almost mistakes for healing before it settles into something else. You catalog this. You do not know what to do with the catalog yet.
loss_variants.scholar:
The wolf was not fighting you. This is the conclusion you reach while you are on your back on the road, which is an uncomfortable time to reach a conclusion but an unavoidable one. It was testing you — a systematic engagement, probing and withdrawing, measuring your responses until it found the one it was looking for. Then it put you down.

It stands over you now, and this is the part that your notes will return to repeatedly. It is not behaving as a predator with downed prey. It is behaving as an instrument completing a calibration. The amber eyes are not hungry. They are reading. You lie still because your training says that stillness is data-gathering, and also because you cannot do anything else.

The wolf breathes out and steps back. The assessment is complete. You have been documented.

The corruption enters through the bruises while you are cataloguing the sensation — a warmth that your instruments would register as cellular and your body registers as almost pleasant, which is the most unsettling part. You write nothing for a full minute. Then you write everything.
loss_variants.scout:
You read the fight wrong. That is the honest accounting. You read it as a predator operating on instinct and it was operating on something else — patience, coordination, an awareness of your patterns that suggested it had been watching you for longer than the encounter. It put you down efficiently, no excess force, and that economy of effort is itself information you did not have when you were still standing.

It stands over you. You do a ground-level threat assessment without moving, which is the only kind available to you right now: wolf, large, injured but not enough to matter, standing with the stillness of something that is not still from exhaustion. Amber eyes. The quality of its attention is not predatory. It is not hungry. It is checking something.

Then it steps back. Measured. Gone into the forest before you have fully decided to move.

The corruption is in the bruises before you sit up — you feel it the way you feel weather changing, a pressure-shift deep in the tissue, warmth that belongs to something other than your own blood. You note the sensation with the same care you would give any new data about a hostile environment. Something has marked you. You do not yet know what for.

acts.title: The Others Arrive
acts.subtitle: You are not alone.
acts.mechanic_hint: Your Companions tab shows who is with you and their relationship level. It changes as you travel together.
acts.text:
The second portal tear opens at dusk — smaller than the original, less controlled, the edges fraying as it closes. Saoirse comes through first.

She doesn't say anything. She scans the tree line from left to right, then right to left, then looks at you and nods once. This is, you will come to understand, a complete sentence.

Mireille follows twenty seconds later, opening her field instruments before her feet are fully through. "Interesting," she says, examining a reading. "The portal dispersion pattern suggests targeted interference. Someone separated us deliberately." She pauses. "Or something."

Tierlan comes through last, sideways, looking back over his shoulder at wherever he was for the previous hours. He takes in the waystation, the tree line, your expression, and the road ahead in approximately three seconds.

"Right," he says. "So. Camp?"

You make camp. The waystation is defensible, which is the only thing that matters tonight.

variants.warrior:
The portal tear opens at dusk and you are already facing it. You have been facing the likely re-entry point for the last forty minutes, because the alternative was facing a direction that offered you nothing actionable.

Saoirse comes through first. She clears the threshold in a single step, weapon-hand loose, eyes already on the perimeter. She scans left to right, then catches your eyes and nods once. Warrior to warrior. The situation is understood. You both let it be understood without saying anything about it.

Mireille follows, instruments already live, and delivers her analysis before she has taken a second step: targeted portal interference, deliberate separation. You file this. It means something organized you, and something organized is a threat with a chain of command, and a chain of command has a point of failure somewhere.

Tierlan arrives last and sideways, and his three-second assessment of the scene produces the only practical question left to ask.

"Camp?" he says.

You take the waystation because it is defensible. You assign watches without being asked. This is what you know how to do when everything else is unclear.
variants.scholar:
You have been calculating the probability of a second portal emergence for the last hour, adjusting the estimate against atmospheric conditions and the resonance drift you recorded when the first one closed. The tear that opens at dusk is roughly consistent with your median projection, which is more comfort than it has any right to be.

Saoirse comes through first. She reads the scene in one pass — you, the waystation, the tree line — and nods. No wasted words. You note her methodology with professional appreciation.

Mireille is through twenty seconds later, instruments running, and she confirms your hypothesis before you can share it: deliberate dispersion, targeted interference. "Someone separated us," she says. She is already writing. You resist the urge to compare notebooks immediately, but only barely.

Tierlan's arrival is characteristically economical. He takes three seconds to process everything and arrives at the only question that moves the situation forward.

"Camp?" he says.

You make camp, and Mireille's instruments are calibrated and recording before the fire is lit. You have, between the two of you, already begun building the dataset this place requires.
variants.scout:
You heard the portal forming ten seconds before it opened — a pressure change, a compression in the air that you have now catalogued as the specific signature of a forced tear rather than a clean transit. You were already positioned with sight lines to the emergence point when Saoirse stepped through.

She read the environment before she read you, which you respect. Left to right sweep, tree line, road, then your position, then a single nod. She found the same information you found and reached the same conclusions. You recognize the process.

Mireille's analysis confirms what the portal's behavior already told you: someone separated you deliberately. You had marked this as likely but lacked the instrument data to be certain. Now you are certain, and certainty is a resource.

Tierlan comes through last, already angled wrong relative to wherever he came from, and he does his assessment in three seconds flat. You count. He is not fast for the sake of looking fast. He is simply efficient.

"Camp?" he says.

The waystation is the right call. You were already calculating it. You help establish the perimeter before anyone suggests one, because the dark arrives in this forest with intent and the sooner you know its edges the better.

acts.title: The First Night
acts.subtitle: Rest heals. Learn how.
acts.mechanic_hint: Use Rest at camp to recover HP and reduce fatigue. You must manage supplies — resting costs one day of food.
acts.text:
The fire is smaller than you'd like. Tierlan found the best watchpoint before anyone else thought to look for one, which means the next three hours belong to him and the tree line.

Mireille is writing. She has been writing since before her instruments finished calibrating. The notebook will be full before the Marches are.

Saoirse sits across the fire and doesn't say anything, which is already starting to feel like a consistent feature of her company. She passed you a ration without comment. She checked the perimeter without being asked. She is watching the dark with the particular attention of someone who has watched a great many darks and learned exactly what to look for in each of them.

You are in the Marches. Whatever brought you through that portal is still here. Whatever the wolf was checking you for, you passed — or failed, depending on what it was looking for.

The corruption in the bruises has settled into something that could almost be warmth, if warmth felt like that.

The road is ahead.

variants.warrior:
The fire is small and positioned correctly — Tierlan found the watchpoint with the angles you would have chosen, and you note this about him without saying anything. The watch rotation fell into place without negotiation, which is the best thing you can say about a group of people you have known for less than a day.

You sit with the waystation wall at your back and take stock. The day's engagements: one wolf, which you assessed wrong at least twice before you stopped assessing wrong. One corrupted creature that showed no aggression. One room with evidence of a retreat that went well enough. The corruption in your bruises is the detail you keep returning to — you have trained against corruption, you know what exposure does, and what it is doing now does not match the literature. It feels like the bruises have learned something. You are not sure what.

Saoirse passes you a ration without breaking her watch of the tree line. The gesture has the quality of a professional courtesy. You accept it the same way.

Whatever organized that wolf is still here. Tomorrow you will know more about what it wants. Tonight you will sleep correctly and wake ready. This is what you know how to do.
variants.scholar:
Mireille is writing across the fire, and her pen has not stopped since before camp was fully established. You understand the impulse completely. The amount of data this day has produced is genuinely extraordinary — you have pages already, and there are gaps you cannot fill until you have better instruments and a better baseline understanding of how this region's corruption operates.

The obsidian shard sits at the edge of your field kit, faintly luminous. You have recorded its temperature, its weight, its spectral output, and the way it interacts with your proximity instrument's readings. You have no explanatory framework for any of these measurements. This is uncomfortable in a way you have learned to treat as productive.

The wolf concerns you most. Not the fight — the behavior after. 'Someone looked through its eyes' is the note you keep returning to. It is the kind of observation that sounds dramatic and is, in fact, the most precise description available. Something assessed you through an intermediary and reached a conclusion. That conclusion is now operating somewhere in this forest. You would like very much to know what it was.

The corruption in the bruises is a secondary concern, for now. You are recording the sensation every hour. Baseline is important. So is sleep.
variants.scout:
The fire is smaller than comfort requires and larger than concealment allows, which means Tierlan got the calculus right: this is a fire for a team that needs to function tomorrow, not a fire for people who want to feel safe. You respect it.

You take the first hours as they come and use them to finish reading the ground you did not have time to read during the day. The waystation sits on a slight rise — natural drainage, clear sight lines to the east, blind spot on the northern approach that Tierlan's watchpoint covers. Whoever built this knew what they were building. Whoever stopped maintaining it knew when to leave.

The forest is quiet in the organized way you noted this morning, but the quality of the silence has shifted. Something that was watching has made a decision. You cannot tell yet whether the decision concerns you or has simply moved on to other considerations. The difference matters and you will not know it until morning at the earliest.

The corruption in your bruises tells you something about the air here — it settled the same way weather settles, a pressure finding equilibrium. This is information. You do not yet know what it is information about.

Saoirse's watch ends clean. The road is still there when dawn comes.

mechanical.note: Full rest applied. Day advances to 2.

acts.title: Something Is Tracking You
acts.subtitle: Day 2. A real fight.
acts.mechanic_hint: This is a real combat encounter. Your choices and stats determine the outcome. Winning or losing both advance the story — but losing costs corruption.
acts.setup_text:
The companions broke camp at first light. Two hours east on the road, the forest thins into a clearing that the maps don't include — thirty meters of bare stone and frost, no cover, and at the far edge a figure that is not a wolf but moves like one.

A Marches Stalker. Corrupted, bonded to the terrain, fast. It has been following you since before you noticed it, which means it has been following you since yesterday.

Saoirse takes one step forward and stops when you put a hand up. She checks the tree line instead. This one is yours.

setup_variants.warrior:
Two hours into the morning march, the road opens into a clearing the maps show as solid forest. Thirty meters of exposed stone. At the far edge: a Marches Stalker, crouched and patient, with the quality of attention of something that has been watching you long enough to form opinions.

It has tracked you through the night. You know this the way you know terrain — by what the ground tells you, the parallel trail in the frost that has been maintaining distance since yesterday's waystation. It wanted you to see it now. The choice to engage in the open is yours.

Saoirse takes a half-step forward. You raise one hand. She stops. Reads the situation. Moves to the tree line to cover your flank. She does not need to be told.
setup_variants.scholar:
The clearing is an anomaly — your records show no such gap in the Wolf-Road forest cover for this grid. Something or someone cleared it. The frost on the stone is uniform, undisturbed, which means whatever is standing at the far edge came from the opposite direction.

A Marches Stalker. Your preparatory reading described them as territorial but not aggressive unless cornered. This one is neither cornered nor, apparently, respecting that research.

Mireille's instrument pings twice behind you. She is already reading it. "The corruption signature matches what we found in the wolf," she says quietly. "Same pattern. Same source."

Same source. You had written 'someone' in your notes. This is what 'someone' sends.
setup_variants.scout:
You spotted the parallel trail in yesterday's frost before you cleared the waystation. It has been maintaining seventy meters of distance through the night, which means it is disciplined rather than merely cautious. You have been tracking something that was tracking you back.

Now it is in a clearing it chose, at a distance it chose, at a time it chose. It is not ambushing you. It is presenting itself. The distinction matters: you are being offered a fight, not taken by one.

You signal Tierlan to the right flank before you speak to anyone. He is already moving.

acts.win_text:
The Stalker goes still the moment it loses the ground — not injured enough to explain the cessation, simply done. It turns its head to one side, tracking something you cannot see or hear, and then it withdraws into the forest with the deliberate economy of a creature that has received new instructions.

It did not fight to win. You understand this in the moment after, the way you understand things you do not want to understand.

Saoirse appears at your shoulder. She does not say anything. She watches the tree line for thirty seconds, then nods once and moves on.

win_variants.warrior:
The Stalker goes still before the final exchange — not a retreat, a cessation, as if something told it to stop. You are already mid-strike when it disengages, and you have to pull the blow because an opponent that is no longer fighting is not something you want on your conscience.

It turns its head. Not at you. At something past you, something in the direction from which it came. Then it goes, the same deliberate withdrawal you saw in the wolf yesterday. Same pattern. Same receiver.

Saoirse is at your shoulder by the time the tree line closes over it. She watches the forest for a count of thirty. Her hand is still on her weapon.

"That was a message," she says. The first full sentence she has offered in twenty-four hours. You both know what it means.
win_variants.scholar:
The Stalker's disengagement is abrupt and total — one moment it is pressing, the next it is still, with the quality of a mechanism that has been switched off rather than an animal that has decided to retreat. The differentiation is significant. You are already recording it when it withdraws.

Mireille is beside you within seconds. "Same cessation pattern as the wolf," she says, before you have voiced the observation. "Something called it off." She pauses. "Something that was watching the fight. Something that now has new information about you."

You write: 'Day 2, morning. Second controlled withdrawal. Assessment still in progress. Ours or theirs?' You underline the question mark.
win_variants.scout:
The Stalker stops fighting when it is still capable of continuing. This is the data point you carry forward: it was not beaten to the point of withdrawal. Something withdrew it. You caught the exact moment — a cessation that started in the head rather than the body, the quality of an animal receiving a command mid-engagement.

Tierlan materializes from the tree line to your right. He watched the whole exchange from cover and his expression is the specific one you have learned means he is filing a large amount of information without making it visible.

"Disciplined," he says. One word. A complete analysis.

acts.loss_text:
The Stalker is better than you today. This is the accounting: faster, more coordinated, and it did not fight like something trying to win. It fought like something trying to establish a fact, and the fact is now established.

You are on the frost. Your HP is not zero — it stopped before that, with the same deliberate patience as the wolf yesterday. It stands over you for four seconds. You count them. Then it goes.

The corruption enters through the new wounds like it belongs there. Saoirse helps you to your feet without comment. Her grip is stronger than you remember.

loss_variants.warrior:
You lost. The honest accounting: you lost to something that was not trying to kill you, which makes the loss more unsettling rather than less. It beat you to a specific point — below, not out — and then it stopped, the same deliberate cessation you saw in the wolf. Same protocol. Same patience.

You are on the frost. The Stalker stands over you for four seconds of the most structured attention you have received from a non-human entity in your career. Then it withdraws.

Saoirse helps you up. Her grip finds the angle that does not pull against the wounds, which means she assessed the damage before she reached for you. She does not say anything. The tree line is clear. The road continues.

The corruption is in the new wounds before you have fully processed the loss. It feels, as it did yesterday, like something settling in.
loss_variants.scholar:
The defeat provides data. You attempt to find comfort in this and partially succeed.

The Stalker's combat patterns matched the wolf's: systematic assessment, escalating pressure, deliberate cessation at a specific threshold below fatal. This is a protocol, not predation. Someone has designed a test with a specific stopping criterion, and that criterion is apparently 'down but not dead.'

You are on the frost. The Stalker completes its four-second assessment and leaves. Mireille is beside you before you have finished sitting up, instruments already recording your biometrics. "Corruption uptake is accelerating," she says. "The wounds are acting as entry points." She says it clinically. You appreciate the clinical framing.

The corruption in the new wounds is warmer than the bruises were yesterday. The warmth is becoming familiar. You note this with concern.
loss_variants.scout:
You read the fight right this time and lost anyway, which is a specific and instructive kind of defeat. The Stalker was operating to a standard you have not encountered before — disciplined, patient, willing to absorb damage in order to hold position — and when it had established what it came to establish, it stopped.

Four seconds standing over you. You read the posture: not predatory, not triumphant. Something receiving a reading. Then it goes.

Tierlan has your arm before you ask. He found the angle that doesn't pull the shoulder, which means he scouted the landing before he reached for you. He doesn't make anything of it.

The corruption in the new wounds has a different character from yesterday's — faster to settle, warmer, as if the tissue already knows the process. You note this. You note that you are beginning to accumulate notes of a kind you did not expect to be taking.

acts.title: Day 3. Something Is Coming.
acts.subtitle: The tutorial is complete.
acts.mechanic_hint: The Map shows your route through the Marches. Each node is a region to travel through. Your choices affect what you encounter.
acts.text:
Day 3 arrives with the quality of something that has been waiting.

The road has changed overnight — not physically, but in the way the forest relates to it. The trees are farther back. The light reaches the stone more directly. The organized silence of the first two days has been replaced by something less organized and therefore more honest: actual quiet, the kind that means nothing is currently watching you, rather than the kind that means something has decided to be patient.

You have been in the Marches for three days. You have been watched, tested, and assessed. Something out there has been building a picture of you — your capabilities, your responses, your limits. Whatever comes next, it will come informed.

Saoirse walks beside you and watches the road ahead. Mireille has stopped writing, which is the most unusual thing that has happened in three days. Tierlan has moved to the front of the group without announcing it.

The road continues east. At dusk, you will learn who has been watching.

variants.warrior:
Three days of assessment. You have been a soldier long enough to know what assessment looks like, and what you have experienced in the Marches is not random corruption — it is a systematic evaluation with the structure of a campaign: probing, escalating pressure, deliberate restraint at key moments.

Someone is building intelligence on you. Someone with enough reach to coordinate corrupted creatures, enough patience to take three days, and enough interest in your specific person to not simply kill you.

You walk the road with this knowledge and find that knowing it doesn't help as much as you would like it to. Saoirse has taken the forward position without being asked. This means she has read the same situation and arrived at the same conclusion: something is coming, and the road is the only option available.

You have been in worse positions than 'walking toward a known threat.' You have, in fact, been in almost no other kind.
variants.scholar:
Three days of data. You have more than you know what to do with, and several observations that your existing frameworks have no category for. The corruption's behavior. The creatures' coordination. The deliberate restraint — two encounters that stopped short of maximum damage. Two assessments conducted through controlled proxies.

Someone is running an experiment. You are the subject variable.

Mireille has stopped writing, which means she has reached the limit of what instruments can tell her without a new input. She needs the next data point as much as you do, and the next data point will provide itself at dusk. You walk toward it with the specific discomfort of a researcher who understands the methodology of the experiment they are inside.

The obsidian shard has been warm for three days. It is warmer today. You note the progression.
variants.scout:
Three days. You have a complete read on the environment now: the organized silence, the assessment protocol, the deliberate restraint at the point of maximum advantage. This is not wilderness predation. This is surveillance with a specific objective, conducted by something patient and resourceful enough to run two test encounters without tipping the full hand.

You know what it looks like when you are being prepared for something. You have run enough approach operations to recognize the structure from the inside.

At dusk, the preparation ends. The objective arrives.

Tierlan is at the front without discussion. Saoirse has taken the rear. Mireille is between you and Tierlan, instruments dark — she stopped recording, which means she considers the current data sufficient for whatever comes next. You are, all four of you, as ready as three days of relentless assessment have made you.

The road runs east. You follow it.

mechanical.note: Tutorial complete. Prologue ends. Kasyrra encounter triggers at dusk.

skip_text: The road begins where it began for those who came before you. The waystation is behind you. Your companions are with you. Kasyrra found you three days ago and left a mark you cannot explain. The Marches are ahead.
